text:
The Matildas have been dealt a brutal reality check in their opening match of the 2024 Paris Olympics after going down convincingly to Germany.

The Matildas' push for a medal at the 2024 Paris Olympics has been dealt a major blow after they were battered by Germany in their opening match.

Germany was always going to be a tough prospect for the Aussies to face, however the ease of which the Matildas were ultimately cast aside will be a concern for coach Tony Gustavsson.

The fourth-ranked nation in women's football badly exposed Australia's defence and they found the breakthrough in the 24th minute when defender Marina Hegering headed the ball past Mackenzie Arnold off a corner.

While the Matildas had chances in attack, they struggled to be clinical without main talisman Sam Kerr and with in-form striker Michelle Heyman benched from the start.

Across the game, they registered six shots and just three on target as they failed to regularly test German keeper Ann-Katrin Berger.

They also held a slender lead in possession over the 90 minutes, but they were unable to create any meaningful opportunities with the ball.

Australia was later made to pay by their opponents, who took advantage of their own attacking opportunities to take themselves out of reach.

The Matildas' shaky set piece defence was punished once more in the 64th minute with striker Lea Schuller's header getting past Arnold on this occasion.

Just four minutes later, winger Jule Brand, who had caused havoc for the Matildas throughout the game, put the match to bed when she finished off a fluid Germany move that cut out the Australian defence to make the score 3-0.

Aussie captain Steph Catley said the result was "definitely a wake-up call".

"We have two more games and we have to turn it around quickly. We have a lot to learn from here. I think at times we were well on top and let two set pieces affect the game in a very negative way. There are things that we can fix nice and quickly, which is good. But we just need to be better," she said.

"They are a good team. We did it to ourselves in the end. We conceded two set pieces. We are usually much, much better on set pieces. It is a wake-up call and it is not to our standard and we need to be better for the next game and turn this tournament around.”

Australia have Zambia in their next match, a must-win encounter as they still have the fifth-ranked United States to face.

A win and a draw in their remaining two games would almost certainly be enough for Australia to progress to the quarter-finals.

The top two of each of the three groups qualify to the quarter-finals, as well as the two best third-placed teams across the groups.
